availability.p: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Deed Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License.

All citation, reuse or distribution of this work must contain a link back to DOI: https://doi.org/10.60760/unibo/igcyrgvcyr2 and the filename (IGCyr000000 or GVCyr000), as well as the year of consultation.

support.p: Four joining fragments of a Parian marble half column, produced through sawing vertically a fluted column and so producing a new, slightly tapering surface; the left side has a flat margin of 0.08 before the first fluting; the other half column was used for IGCyr065200 ( 0.53-0.59 2.75 -0.22 ).

layoutDesc.layout: Inscribed: ll. 1-2 on the whole width; from l. 3 on in two columns, each entry ending with a numeral, preceded by a space after the personal name, if needed.

handDesc.handNote: 0.016-0.02 (min E to max I) at ll. 1-2, 0.08 from l. 3 on; serifs.

origin.origDate: Between ca. 280 BC (Laronde) and ca. 270 BC (Rosamilia)

provenance.p: Found before 1938 at Cyrene: exact findspot unrecorded.
Seen in 1979 by C. Dobias-Lalou in Shahat: Cyrene Museum.
Main fragment seen by E. Rosamilia in 2012 at the same place.

encodingDesc.p: Marked-up according to the EpiDoc Guidelines version 8

revisionDesc.change: added ref to the first edition
checked, updated and in case corrected; added Italian translation
checked, updated and in case corrected physDesc, history, and textClass
lemma, metadata, bibliography, text edited, apparatus, commentary

div.ab:
Ἱαρεὺς Νικόβωλος Ἰάσονος. .
ὅσσοι ἐπέδωκαν χρήματα τᾶι πόλει·

rdg.note: corrigenda by Pugliese Carratelli at p. 368

app.note: all letters of the name lost, but the summits of αλ or αδ under the first round letter of l. 5

This list of contributors has been cut in the same workshop and by the same hand as IGCyr065200; a third list with a similar, but somewhat smaller lettering is IGCyr097170, while a fragment recorded in 1861 but now lost (IGCyr009300) cannot be attributed to anyone of the three lists because of lack of information. Nothing is known about its discovery, whereas Oliverio mentioned that of its pendant IGCyr065200. The main part of this one was plausibly re-used in a context where it was not exposed to be worn out and its state of preservation is remarkably good. The additional fragments were probably found after World War II, but we have no information about this.
The incipit is restored through collation with IGCyr065200.
We do not know the purpose of the contributions; families often gave together, so that a father is followed by his sons (and sometimes grandsons) and/or brother(s).

For the names at col. i, ll. 16-17, the plausible restorations are based on the fact that the rare name Ἀρθμιάδας is known from tomb S388 where a base of early second century BC has Ἀρθμιάδας Εὐφράνορος (IGCyr085000). It is therefore highly plausible that those lines mention an ancestor and his son; the evidence for the date is based on the lettering and mostly on the prosopraphical studies of Laronde and now of Rosamilia.

The name at col. i, l. 105 is not easy to restore. *Θιναμύας reluctantly proposed in has no parallel. The most resembling forms are Carian names in -αμυης (see , p. 21), whence Παναμυας at Dorian Cos. C. Dobias-Lalou now suggests a reading Μιναμύας,although it does not match exactly 's observation of traces compatible with either kappa, chi or a triangular character.

At col. ii, l. 5 the preserved letters certainly belong to a name ending with -]ίοχος. This might belong to a first name with a long first member, followed by a short father's name. However, no instance is at hand, whereas Ἁνίοχος (or even Ἀντίοχος) are better attested in Cyrenaica. So it seems more plausible to suspect a short first name followed by the father's name Ἁν]ιόχω.
